serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass (300 g) of carrot juice drink, as commonly served in Singapore food courts and cafes.

nutrient_source.title: Carrot juice, canned
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for carrot juice. Cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central entry for carrot juice (FDC ID 167762) and are used to supplement HPB data. The product is a sweetened carrot juice drink made from concentrate; values are representative of this preparation style.

gi_source.title: Carrot juice, freshly made — GI value 43 (glucose reference)
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI of 43 (glucose reference) for freshly made carrot juice. This sweetened carrot juice drink made from concentrate is expected to have a similar or slightly higher GI due to added sugars; GI 43 is used as a conservative lower-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (43 × 30.42) / 100 ≈ 13.1 for a 300 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.14 g/100 g) in a beverage form; sugar-sweetened beverages independently raise blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance, despite low sodium (16 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this sweetened carrot juice drink due to its high sugar content (9.14 g/100 g), which exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold and independently elevates blood pressure risk; prefer whole carrots or unsweetened carrot juice to retain fibre and reduce glycaemic and cardiovascular impact.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 9.14 g/100 g in liquid form with minimal dietary fibre (0.36 g/100 g); juicing concentrates natural sugars and removes fibre, resulting in rapid glucose absorption and elevated postprandial glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this carrot juice drink because its high sugar content (9.14 g/100 g) and very low fibre (0.36 g/100 g) produce a rapid postprandial glucose spike; if consumed, restrict to no more than 100 ml and pair with a protein or fat source to attenuate absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added and concentrated sugars (9.14 g/100 g) in a beverage contribute to elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; fat content is negligible (0.03 g/100 g) and cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g, which are positive factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this sweetened carrot juice drink due to its high sugar content (9.14 g/100 g), which can raise triglycerides and contribute to metabolic cardiovascular risk; the negligible fat and zero cholesterol are favourable, but the sugar load outweighs these benefits in a beverage context.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Carrot juice drink is a processed beverage made from carrot juice concentrate diluted with water and sweetened with added sugars. It has a mildly sweet, earthy flavour characteristic of carrots, with a thin, watery texture compared to freshly pressed carrot juice. The product is widely available in Singapore supermarkets, food courts, and convenience stores. Per 100 g, it provides 42 kcal, 0.23 g protein, 0.03 g fat, and 10.14 g carbohydrate of which 9.14 g is sugar, with very low dietary fibre (0.36 g) and low sodium (16 mg). The high sugar-to-fibre ratio classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), which is clinically relevant for type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease management. Unlike whole carrots (GI ~35, fibre ~2.8 g/100 g), the juiced and sweetened form concentrates sugars and removes most fibre, increasing the glycaemic impact. Vitamin C is present at 5 mg/100 g. The beverage is vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. A standard serving is one glass (300 g), providing approximately 126 kcal and 30.4 g carbohydrate.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a glass of carrot juice drink?
Is carrot juice drink good for blood pressure?
What is the sugar content of carrot juice drink?
Is carrot juice drink suitable for weight loss?
Carrot juice drink nutrition facts per 100g

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (confidence 0.95 per input metadata). Secondary nutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central carrot juice data. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry. Overall data quality is high for this food category.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this carrot juice drink product is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central carrot juice entry as of 2026. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB source entry for this product and are not available in a directly matching approved source entry as of 2026. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central carrot juice data; carrot juice products do not contain industrially produced trans fats. calcium_mg is 0.0 and iron_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for carrot juice. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero; carrot juice is a plant-derived beverage and inherently contains no cholesterol. The input record listed cholesterol, saturated_fat, trans_fat, and vitamin_c as sourced from 'grabfood' (a forbidden delivery platform source); these values have been re-validated against USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 167762) and are consistent with approved source data. The GI value in the input (39) was sourced from an 'Embedded GI Database' with confidence 0.5; this has been replaced with GI 43 from the University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate for freshly made carrot juice) to comply with approved GI source policy. The input health_suitability entries referenced 'rheumatoid_arthritis' which is not a mandatory condition under Veritas v2; this has been replaced with the three mandatory conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease). Dietary tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from input cultural section are preserved in classification dietary_flags context. Portion size of 300 g (1 glass) retained from input portions data.
